content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 7, 1999




Roy A. Hanley
City Attorney
City of Atascadero
1104 B Vine Street
Paso Robles, California 93446

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-99-187

Dear Mr. Hanley:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Atascadero
Councilmember Jerry Clay regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that the Commission does not act as finder of fact when rendering advice.
This advice is applicable and confers immunity to the extent that the facts provided are correct
and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     Does Councilmember Clay, who is also a real estate agent, have a conflict of interest in a
decision proposed by Dan Mackey to approve a general plan and zoning map amendment and
subdivision to create 30 small homes on small residential lots to be sold to low income buyers?

CONCLUSION

     No. Councilmember Clay does not have a conflict of interest in any decision concerning
Mr. Mackey's project.


_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-187
Page 2

FACTS

Jerry Clay is a licensed real estate agent. His license is currently with York Real Estate. Within the last 12 months, his license was with Peabody and Plum. Kelly Gearhart is a builder in town who lists most of his Atascadero property through Peabody and Plum. Councilmember Clay has never sold nor represented a purchaser in one of Mr. Gearhart's properties. Councilmember Clay does not have an ownership interest in Peabody and Plum.

There is currently pending at the planning commission level an application by Dan Mackey for a general plan and zoning map amendment and subdivision to create 30 small homes on small residential lots. The homes are only to be sold to low income buyers. Mr. Gearhart is representing Mr. Mackey in the application process free of charge because they are good friends. Mr. Mackey is not a source of income to Councilmember Clay.

Mr. Gearhart also owns a piece of property known as the Rochelle property farther north in Atascadero. This property is more than 2,500 feet from Mr. Mackey's project. Mr. Gearhart proposes to make a commercial development on the Rochelle property. Mr. Mackey's project would alter the urban services line. The request is to extend the urban services line to include Mr. Mackey's site and make it possible to extend sewer mains. A staff report indicates that there is adequate plant capacity for Mr. Mackey's project. There is also adequate plant capacity for Mr. Gearhart's Rochelle commercial project.

Although Mr. Gearhart has obtained all of the approvals necessary for his commercial project, there is a condition of that approval that relates to the extension of the sewer line. The sewer line is not currently extended to the Rochelle property. Mr. Gearhart's project is conditioned upon the sewer line being extended to his project. Mr. Gearhart has not yet decided which of many alternative routes or methods will be used to extend the sewer line. The extension of the sewer line to Mr. Mackey's property would allow Mr. Gearhart to elect a sewer extension plan to the Rochelle property that may be less expensive than other alternatives. However, you do not know whether Mr. Gearhart would select that alternative. The Rochelle property is not currently listed with Peabody and Plum, nor do you believe that the Rochelle property will be listed with Peabody and Plum since another person currently has an option to buy the Rochelle property.

The Mackey proposal involves 30 homes. There are other bigger projects in Atascadero. Councilmember Clay will not sell any of the Mackey homes nor will he refer buyers to any other agent concerning the Mackey homes. He does sell homes in the city, but will not sell, refer or list for Mr. Mackey. Neither Peabody and Plum nor York Real Estate will receive listings on any of the 30 homes.

File No. A-99-187
Page 3

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or in any way
attempting to use their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A councilmember is a public official for purposes of the
Act. (Section 82048.)

Economic Interests

An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on, among other enumerated "economic interests": (1) any source of
income of $250 or more provided to, received by or promised to the official within 12 months
prior to the time the decision is made; or (2) any business entity in which the official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds any position of management. (Section
87103(c) and (d).)

The following persons are considered to be "sources of income" to real estate agents:
(1) the broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the agent works; (2) the
person the agent represents in the transaction; and (3) any person who receives a finder's or other
referral fee for referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant
to a contract with the broker. (Regulation 18703.3(c)(3)(C).)

Councilmember Clay is a real estate agent, who has been licensed with York Real Estate
and Peabody and Plum within the last 12 months. Thus, York Real Estate and Peabody and
Plum are both sources of income to the councilmember. Presumably, he has received $250 or
more within the previous 12 months from both entities. As an employee, Councilmember Clay
also has an economic interest in York Real Estate pursuant to Section 87103(d).

Although Mr. Gearhart lists most of his property through Peabody and Plum,
Councilmember Clay has never represented Mr. Gearhart in any transaction, nor has he ever had
an ownership interest in Peabody and Plum.² Under these facts, Mr. Gearhart is not a source of
income to the councilmember. (Clay Advice Letter, No. I-96-335.)

Accordingly, the councilmember may not make, participate in making or use his official
position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on York Real Estate or Peabody and Plum.

² The term "income" includes the pro rata share of any income of any business entity in which the
individual owns a 10 percent interest or greater. (Section 82030(a).) Thus, clients of a business are considered
sources of income to those who have a 10 percent interest or greater in the business.

File No. A-99-187
Page 4

Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the official's economic interest?

Once the official identifies his or her economic interest, the official must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interest. First, the official must determine whether the economic interest will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. Based upon the type of involvement, the official must then apply the appropriate standard to ascertain whether the financial impact of the decision will be material. After the official finds the pertinent materiality standard, he or she must decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met.

1. Direct or Indirect Involvement

A business entity is directly involved in a decision if the entity is the named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) A business entity is the subject of a decision if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or contract with the person. (Id.) If a business entity is not directly involved in the decision, it is indirectly involved for purposes of applying the materiality standards.

Work Real Estate and Peabody and Plum are indirectly involved in any decision involving Mr. Mackey's project.

2. Materiality Standard

For business entities that are indirectly involved in a decision, the pertinent materiality standard is set forth in Regulation 18705.1(b). The materiality thresholds in the regulation vary depending upon the size of the business entity. For relatively small businesses indirectly involved in a decision, the effect of a decision is material if it will result in an increase or decrease in: (1) gross revenues of $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; (2) existing expenses of $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities of $10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(b)(7).)

3. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?

An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty as a result of the decision, but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) With this in mind, the important question may now be framed: Is it substantially likely that a decision regarding Mr. Mackey's project will affect York Real Estate or Peabody and Plum by the appropriate amount specified in Regulation 18705.1(b)(7)?

File No. A-99-187
Page 5

The Mackey proposal involves 30 homes. Councilmember Clay will not sell any of the
Mackey homes nor will he refer buyers to another agent concerning the Mackey homes.
Councilmember Clay will not sell, refer or list for Mr. Mackey. In addition, neither York Real
Estate nor Peabody and Plum will receive listings on any of the 30 homes. Because there is no
indication that the business activity of York Real Estate and Peabody and Plum will increase as a
result of Mr. Mackey's project, it does not appear substantially likely that the project will have a
material financial effect on either business entity as described in Regulation 18705.1(b)(7).

You mention that the Mackey project may benefit Mr. Gearhart's Rochelle property by
extending the sewer line. In addition, Mr. Gearhart lists all of his Atascadero property through
Peabody and Plum. However, the property is not currently listed with Peabody and Plum, nor do
you believe that it will be since another person presently has an option to purchase the property.
Because there is no indication that Peabody and Plum will be involved in any transaction
affecting the ownership of the Rochelle property, it does not appear substantially likely that the
Mackey project will have a material financial effect on Peabody and Plum on the basis that it will
benefit the Rochelle property.

If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Mr. Mackey’s project.Page 2

income to the councilmember. (Clay Advice Letter, No. 1-96-335.)

2
The term “income” includes the pro rata share of any income of any business entity in which the

sources of income to those who have a 10 percent interest or greater in the business.

York Real Estate and Peabody and Plum are indirectly involved in any decision involving

QUESTION: Does Councilmember Clay, who is also a real estate agent, have a conflict of interest in a
decision proposed by Dan Mackey to approve a general plan and zoning map amendment and
subdivision to create 30 small homes on small residential lots to be sold to low income buyers?

CONCLUSION: No. Councilmember Clay does not have a conflict of interest in any decision concerning
Mr. Mackey’s project.Page 2
